metadata.summary: Optimize K-factor by decomposing into i (invites/user) and c (invitee conversion). Fix the weaker leg first. Requires measured baseline K and full event chain tracked.

content.title: Viral Loop Optimization
content.summary: Viral loop optimization iterates on an existing measured K-factor by decomposing it into its two drivers — i (invites sent per user) and c (invitee conversion rate) — identifying the bottleneck, and running A/B tests on the weakest leg. Optimize c first (invite landing page) before optimizing i (share moments); every improvement to c multiplies all future loop turns.

why.p: K-factor is a compound metric: K = i * c. A small improvement to the lower leg often has larger absolute impact than a large improvement to the higher one. Without funnel decomposition, optimization effort is directed by intuition rather than math. Cycle time is a secondary lever — halving cycle time roughly squares compounding over a quarter.

list.item: Product has a working invite/share path and a measured baseline K (even K = 0.05 is enough to start).
Full event chain is tracked: invite_shown → invite_sent → invite_clicked → invitee_signup → invitee_active.
A live A/B test framework is available (no code deploy needed to split variants).
At least 5,000 weekly users entering the loop (otherwise sample size kills experiments).
Pre-PMF: optimizing K of a product nobody loves yields K = 0.0X times any multiplier — still nothing.
B2B enterprise where sharing is gated by procurement or security — viral mechanics do not translate.
Markets with strict anti-spam rules (regulated finance, medical, EU consumer) where contact-import is non-compliant.
Products where the inviter's value requires the invitee to join but the invitee has no standalone reason — forced virality churns invitees fast.

section.name: k-factor-formula
section.p: Decompose K into i (avg invites sent per user) and c (invitee conversion rate = signups / invites sent) before deciding where to optimize; optimize c first.

rule.statement: Improving c lifts every future loop turn because all future invites benefit from higher conversion; improving i only increases volume into the current conversion rate.
rule.rationale: K = i * c. A small improvement to the lower leg often has larger absolute impact than a large improvement to the higher one.

list.item: K = i * c
i = total invite_sent events / distinct active users in period
c = invitee_signup events matched to invite tokens / total invite_sent events
cycle_time = median days between invite_sent and invitee_signup

rule.statement: Measure cycle time (p50 days from invite_sent to invitee_signup) and treat it as a primary optimization lever alongside i and c.
rule.rationale: K = 0.4 with 3-day cycle time beats K = 0.7 with 60-day cycle time over a quarter in compounding terms.

list.item: reduce cycle time by: one-click mobile signup, pre-filled email, social login for invitees
track cycle_time weekly; a sudden increase signals friction introduced in a signup change

section.name: funnel-metrics
section.p: Track the full event chain at every stage:

list.item: invite_shown: % of active users who see the invite prompt
invite_sent: % of users who actually send an invite (= share rate)
invite_clicked: clicks on the invite link by recipients
invitee_signup: recipients who complete signup
invitee_active: invitees who reach the activation milestone within D7

rule.statement: Track invitee D30 retention separately from organic D30; if invitee retention is less than 50% of organic, the loop is poisoning the cohort.
rule.rationale: Incentivized loops attract users who join for the reward, not the product; high K with low invitee retention grows the user count but collapses LTV.

rule.statement: Place the share prompt at peak-emotion moments (just completed a milestone, just discovered something great) — not in settings menus.
rule.rationale: Share prompts in settings rely on users seeking them out; achievement-moment prompts capture naturally high intent and produce 3-5x higher share rates.

list.item: achievement trigger (completed goal, hit milestone) → celebration modal with share button
discovery trigger (found something valuable) → "share with a friend who'd love this"
social trigger (want to connect) → "invite teammates to collaborate"
help trigger (need someone else to use it) → collaborative feature invite
achievement triggers — easy / high impact
multi-channel sharing (email, SMS, copy link) — medium / medium
viral content (shareable badges, results images) — medium / high
contact import (bulk invite) — hard / high (privacy constraints; iOS/Android blocked post-2021)
team features that require collaboration — hard / very high

rule.statement: Personal invites ("Anna invited you to [Product]") beat brand invites by 20-40% on click and signup; implement with a single DB join on the referral token.
rule.rationale: Named social proof reduces perceived spam risk and creates accountability, increasing click-through before the landing page is even reached.

list.item: personalize invite landing: show inviter name + avatar + their activity context
add social proof: "join N people using [Product]"
one-click signup: Google/Apple SSO, pre-filled email from invite token
show value before signup: preview of what the invitee will access
mobile-optimize: reduce tap count to 3 or fewer from invite link to activated account

antipattern.description: Requiring signup to view shared content.
antipattern.reason: Forcing pre-signup registration drops invitee conversion by 40-60%; a preview wall converts better than a hard gate.

rule.statement: Reward at value-realization (invitee reaches activation milestone), not at signup, to cut incentive fraud.
rule.rationale: Signup-triggered rewards are easy to game with fake accounts; activation-triggered rewards require genuine engagement that fraud bots cannot mimic at scale.

list.item: one-time reward: credit after invitee activates
tiered multi-referral: 1 referral → small reward, 5 → bigger reward + badge, 10 → large reward + premium feature
two-sided: both inviter and invitee receive reward — higher acceptance rate
cap one-time rewards but tier multi-referral to capture power-referrers

rule.statement: Instrument a fraud-checker subagent to review referrer-IP / device-graph anomalies before any reward is issued on a new incentive structure.
rule.rationale: Incentivized loops attract self-referrers and bot signups; raw K looks great while real LTV plummets — optimizing without fraud feedback maximizes gameable referrals.

list.item: self-referral: inviter and invitee share the same IP, device fingerprint, or email domain
bot signups: invitee accounts with zero post-signup events within 24h
reward farming: single user accumulates more than 10% of all rewards in the period
velocity anomaly: single referrer sends 10x the median invite rate
invitee D7 retention vs organic D7 retention — flag if invitee is less than 50% of organic
invitee LTV vs organic LTV at D90 — if less than 60%, incentive is attracting wrong users
invitee-to-inviter conversion: what % of referred users also invite someone? — secondary K

rule.statement: Anchor K-factor expectations to category benchmarks, not to case studies — consumer apps: K=0.1-0.3; B2B SaaS: K=0.05-0.2.
rule.rationale: Agents over-cite Dropbox/Spotify Wrapped as if any product can replicate them; unrealistic K targets lead to dark-pattern share mechanics that backfire.

list.item: weekly: check K-factor trend, invite funnel drop-offs, top referrers, plan next experiment
monthly: cohort analysis of signups by source, retention of referred vs organic, fraud check, channel performance
quarterly: review loop type effectiveness, major product change to improve virality, incentive structure, competitive analysis

rule.statement: Redesign viral loop mechanics every 12-18 months; users learn the pattern and c decays as familiarity reduces novelty.
rule.rationale: "Personalized invite" wins early then plateaus; design for refresh, not permanence.

section.name: limitations
section.p: Where this methodology fails or does not apply:

list.item: K is fragile to fraud: incentivized loops attract self-referrers and bot signups; "raw K" looks great while real LTV plummets
Cycle time matters as much as K; K=0.4 with 3-day cycle beats K=0.7 with 60-day cycle in compounding
Holdouts are hard to design — network effect of one variant can leak into the control via shared social graphs
"Personalized invite" wins early then plateaus; users learn the pattern; expect decay
One-click contact import is dead in iOS/Android post-2021 privacy changes
Pre-PMF optimization of K of a product nobody loves yields still nothing
B2B enterprise with procurement/security gates: viral mechanics do not translate
Anti-spam regulated markets (finance, medical, EU consumer) where contact-import is non-compliant
Products with forced virality (invitee has no standalone reason to join) churn invitees fast

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Drive viral optimization with subagents in a tight loop: agent reads K-funnel data, proposes 1-3 hypotheses ranked by Impact x Confidence x Ease, writes A/B-test specs from the experiment-spec template, drafts variant copy, then waits for human green-light before any code or live-channel change. After test maturity, agent runs significance check, summarizes lift, and proposes the next experiment.

list.item: growth-marketer (sonnet) — funnel analysis, hypothesis generation, copy drafts
data-analyst (sonnet) — K-factor decomposition (i, c, cycle time), invitee-LTV check
experiment-designer (sonnet) — sample-size math, randomization unit, MDE calc
fraud-checker (sonnet) — referrer-IP / device-graph anomaly review on referral incentives

example.note: Prompt pattern for K-factor analysis and experiment proposal
example.code: Input: viral_funnel_30d.csv (impressions, sends, clicks, signups, activations by variant, by cohort) Task: 1) compute i, c, K, cycle_time per cohort 2) rank top 3 drop-offs by absolute user loss 3) propose ONE A/B test per drop-off using ab-testing-setup template Output: yaml with hypothesis, variant_spec, primary_metric, mde, sample_size, days_required Forbidden: claiming a winner before n >= sample_size AND days >= 14

list.item: growthbook CLI — define experiments, push variant configs; npm i -g @growthbook/cli
statsig CLI — experiment scaffolding, power calc; https://docs.statsig.com/cli
posthog CLI — funnel + cohort export, feature flags; npm i -g posthog-node
branch.io CLI — deferred-deeplink referral attribution; https://help.branch.io
mixpanel-utils — bulk export of invite/conversion events; pip install mixpanel-utils
Viral Loops (SaaS, REST+JS SDK) — templated referral campaigns; agent can draft/clone via API
ReferralCandy (SaaS, REST API) — ecommerce referral, Shopify-native
Friendbuy (SaaS, REST API) — two-sided referral with anti-fraud built in
GrowSurf (SaaS, REST API) — waitlist + referral, lightweight
Branch (SaaS, REST+SDKs) — cross-platform deferred deeplinks for invite attribution
AppsFlyer / Adjust (SaaS, REST) — mobile attribution; needed for K on iOS/Android
GrowthBook (OSS+SaaS, REST+GitOps) — A/B framework, agent can author experiments
Statsig (SaaS, REST+SDKs) — experimentation; CUPED variance reduction
Optimizely (SaaS, partial agent support) — not ideal for agent-driven experiment creation

section.name: k-factor-script
section.p: K-factor decomposition script — compute K and cycle time from event stream CSV:

example.note: k-factor.py — Input: events.csv columns: ts (datetime), type, inviter_id, ref_token. Output: i, c, K, cycle_time median.
example.code:
# k_factor.py — compute K-factor and cycle time from event stream CSV # Input: events.csv with columns: ts (datetime), type, inviter_id, ref_token # Output: i, c, K, cycle_time median printed to stdout
import pandas as pd
ev = pd.read_csv("events.csv", parse_dates=["ts"]) inv = ev[ev.type == "invite_sent"] sig = ev[ev.type == "invitee_signup"]
# Average invites per inviter i = inv.groupby("inviter_id").size().mean()
# Conversion: signups matched to invite tokens matched = sig.merge(inv, on="ref_token", suffixes=("_s", "_i")) c = len(matched) / max(len(inv), 1)
# Cycle time: days from invite_sent to invitee_signup cycle = (matched.ts_s - matched.ts_i).dt.days K = i * c
print(f"i={i:.2f} c={c:.2%} K={K:.3f} cycle_p50={cycle.median():.1f}d")
# Decay check: K by inviter cohort week inv = inv.copy() inv["cohort"] = inv.ts.dt.to_period("W") print("\nInvite volume by cohort:") print(inv.groupby("cohort").size().tail(8).to_string())

example.note: loop_projection.py — naive viral loop compounding model. Shows whether assumed K + cycle time yields self-sustaining growth over a period.
experiment-spec.md — K-Factor experiment spec template (hypothesis, primary metric, MDE, variants, anti-fraud checks). Winner declared only when n >= sample_size AND days >= 14.
example.code:
# K-Factor Experiment: [Name]
## Hypothesis IF we [change to invite flow / landing page / incentive], THEN K-factor will increase by [X%] BECAUSE [mechanism: more share moments / higher conversion / shorter cycle].
## Target Metric - Primary: K-factor (measured over 28-day rolling window) - Secondary: i (invites/user), c (conversion rate), cycle_time_p50
## Variants | Variant | Description | |---------|-------------| | Control | [Current state] | | Treatment | [Proposed change — one change only] |
## Sample Size - Weekly users entering loop: ______ - Baseline K: ______ (i=____, c=____) - MDE relative: ____% on c or i (specify which leg) - n_per_variant: ______ Duration: ______ days
## Anti-Fraud Checks - [ ] Invitee D7 retention vs organic tracked separately - [ ] Self-referral IP/device check configured - [ ] Reward gate: activation required before reward issued
## Decision Winner declared only when: n >= sample_size AND days >= 14

list.item: Optimize c (invitee conversion) before i (invites sent) — improving the landing page lifts every future loop turn
Personal invites ("Anna invited you") beat brand invites by 20-40% on click and signup; cost is one DB join
Reward at value-realization, not at signup — cuts incentive fraud massively
Track invitee D30 retention separately; if less than 50% of organic, the loop is poisoning the cohort
Cap one-time rewards but tier multi-referral (5 referrals → bigger reward, 25 → premium tier) to capture power-referrers
Move share moments to peak emotion (just-completed-a-thing), not to settings menus
K = 0.4 with 3-day cycle beats K = 0.7 with 60-day cycle over a quarter — optimize cycle time too
Agents cite "+125x improvement" case studies as if achievable — anchor to category K: consumer apps 0.1-0.3, B2B SaaS 0.05-0.2
LLMs invent invite-copy claims ("join 50,000 people") that are factually wrong; require numeric claims as inputs, not produced
Agent must check sample size and runtime against MDE before declaring a winner; otherwise it ships noise
Two-sided incentives change unit economics — require pricing-reviewer approval for any new reward structure
Contact-import ideas from LLMs often violate platform TOS or privacy law (CASL, GDPR, CCPA); filter at template stage
Fraud detection is adversarial; agent optimizing without fraud feedback will recommend mechanics that maximize gameable referrals
Andrew Chen, "What's your viral loop?" — https://andrewchen.com/whats-your-viral-loop
Adam Penenberg, "Viral Loop" (book, 2009)
Reforge, "Growth Models" — https://www.reforge.com/blog/growth-loops
Lenny Rachitsky, "How the biggest consumer apps got their first 1000 users" — https://www.lennysnewsletter.com
Jonah Berger, "Contagious: Why Things Catch On" (book)
Branch, "Mobile attribution for viral loops" — https://branch.io/blog
